questions.question: What is a thread in the context of operating systems?
questions.option_a: A. A separate program running independently
questions.option_b: B. A single, independent stream of execution within a process
questions.option_c: C. The main memory allocation unit
questions.option_d: D. A system-level protection mechanism
questions.explanation: A thread represents a single, independent stream of execution within a process/application, allowing multiple tasks to run concurrently within the same process.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the visibility of threads?
questions.option_a: A. Threads are always visible to all system processes
questions.option_b: B. Threads are generally invisible from outside the processes they run within
questions.option_c: C. Threads are visible only to the kernel
questions.option_d: D. Threads are visible only to other threads in different processes
questions.explanation: Threads run within processes and are generally invisible from outside the processes, making them lightweight execution units contained within their parent process.

questions.question: Which statement about process creation versus thread creation is TRUE?
questions.option_a: A. Process creation is lightweight, thread creation is heavyweight
questions.option_b: B. Both process and thread creation require equal resources
questions.option_c: C. Process creation is heavyweight, thread creation is lightweight
questions.option_d: D. Thread creation requires more resources than process creation
questions.explanation: Process creation is heavyweight because many resources need to be allocated, while thread creation is lightweight as threads share resources of the parent process.

questions.question: In a multithreaded process, which of the following is TRUE about thread stacks?
questions.option_a: A. All threads share a single stack
questions.option_b: B. Each thread has its own stack, but threads cannot access each other's stacks
questions.option_c: C. Each thread has its own stack, and threads can read or write any other thread's stack
questions.option_d: D. Stacks are managed entirely by the hardware
questions.explanation: Each thread has its own Program Counter (PC) and Stack, but all threads can access every address in the process, including reading or writing any other thread's stack.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of multithreading according to the lecture?
questions.option_a: A. Responsiveness - continued execution if part of process is blocked
questions.option_b: B. Resource Sharing - threads share resources of the process
questions.option_c: C. Economy - cheaper than process creation
questions.option_d: D. Security - complete isolation between threads
questions.explanation: There is no protection between threads within a process - threads can access each other's memory, which is actually a potential security concern, not a benefit.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of CPU utilization?
questions.option_a: A. Process
questions.option_b: B. Program
questions.option_c: C. Thread
questions.option_d: D. Kernel
questions.explanation: Thread is the basic unit of CPU utilization, as it represents a single stream of execution that can be scheduled to run on a processor.

questions.question: In a uniprocessor system, how many threads can be running at the same time?
questions.option_a: A. Multiple threads, one per process
questions.option_b: B. Only one thread at a time
questions.option_c: C. All threads in the system
questions.option_d: D. All threads in the running process
questions.explanation: In a uniprocessor system, only one thread at a time can be running, while on a multiprocessor, different threads may execute on different processors simultaneously.

questions.question: What happens if one thread is blocked in a multithreaded process?
questions.option_a: A. All threads in the process are blocked
questions.option_b: B. The entire process is terminated
questions.option_c: C. Another thread can run
questions.option_d: D. The system must be rebooted
questions.explanation: Threads within a process are independent - if one thread is blocked, another thread can continue running, providing responsiveness.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes user threads?
questions.option_a: A. Threads managed entirely by the operating system kernel
questions.option_b: B. Threads whose management is done by user-level threads library
questions.option_c: C. Threads that can only run in user mode
questions.option_d: D. Threads that have no kernel counterpart
questions.explanation: User threads are managed by user-level threads libraries, not by the kernel, allowing thread management without operating system intervention.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a user-level thread library?
questions.option_a: A. Windows Kernel
questions.option_b: B. Solaris Kernel Threads
questions.option_c: C. POSIX Pthreads
questions.option_d: D. Linux Scheduler
questions.explanation: POSIX Pthreads is a user-level thread library, along with Windows threads and Java threads, which are mentioned as primary thread libraries.

questions.question: In the Many-to-One threading model, what happens when one thread makes a blocking system call?
questions.option_a: A. Only that thread blocks, others continue
questions.option_b: B. All threads in the process block
questions.option_c: C. The kernel creates a new thread
questions.option_d: D. The process terminates automatically
questions.explanation: In the Many-to-One model, multiple user-level threads are mapped to a single kernel thread, so if one thread blocks, all threads in the process are blocked.

questions.question: Which threading model maps each user-level thread to a kernel thread?
questions.option_a: A. Many-to-One Model
questions.option_b: B. One-to-One Model
questions.option_c: C. Many-to-Many Model
questions.option_d: D. One-to-Many Model
questions.explanation: The One-to-One model maps each user-level thread to a corresponding kernel thread, providing more concurrency than the Many-to-One model.

questions.question: Which operating systems use the One-to-One threading model?
questions.option_a: A. Windows and Linux
questions.option_b: B. Solaris Green Threads only
questions.option_c: C. GNU Portable Threads only
questions.option_d: D. Mac OS 9 only
questions.explanation: Windows and Linux, along with Solaris 9 and later, use the One-to-One threading model where each user thread maps to a kernel thread.

questions.question: What is a limitation of the One-to-One threading model?
questions.option_a: A. No concurrency support
questions.option_b: B. Number of threads per process may be restricted due to overhead
questions.option_c: C. Cannot run on multiprocessor systems
questions.option_d: D. Threads cannot be created dynamically
questions.explanation: In the One-to-One model, the number of threads per process may be restricted due to the overhead of creating a kernel thread for each user thread.

questions.question: Which threading model allows many user-level threads to be mapped to many kernel threads?
questions.option_a: A. Many-to-One Model
questions.option_b: B. One-to-One Model
questions.option_c: C. Many-to-Many Model
questions.option_d: D. Single-to-Single Model
questions.explanation: The Many-to-Many model allows many user-level threads to be mapped to many kernel threads, providing flexibility and concurrency.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a thread pool?
questions.option_a: A. To store terminated threads for debugging
questions.option_b: B. To create a number of threads in a pool where they await work
questions.option_c: C. To limit the total number of threads in the system
questions.option_d: D. To provide a backup for failed threads
questions.explanation: A thread pool creates a number of threads in a pool where they await work, allowing faster service of requests with existing threads rather than creating new ones.

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of using thread pools?
questions.option_a: A. Threads in the pool cannot be reused
questions.option_b: B. Usually slightly faster to service a request with an existing thread than create a new thread
questions.option_c: C. Thread pools require more memory than individual threads
questions.option_d: D. Thread pools automatically delete threads after use
questions.explanation: Thread pools are advantageous because it's usually faster to service a request with an existing thread than to create a new thread, and they allow binding the number of threads to the pool size.

questions.question: In the context of signal handling in multithreaded processes, which approach is mentioned in the lecture?
questions.option_a: A. Always deliver signals to the main thread only
questions.option_b: B. Deliver the signal to the thread to which the signal applies
questions.option_c: C. Never deliver signals to threads
questions.option_d: D. Queue all signals for later processing
questions.explanation: One approach mentioned is to deliver the signal to the thread to which the signal applies, along with other options like delivering to every thread or assigning a specific thread to receive all signals.

questions.question: What is thread cancellation?
questions.option_a: A. Creating a new thread
questions.option_b: B. Terminating a thread before it has finished
questions.option_c: C. Suspending a thread temporarily
questions.option_d: D. Moving a thread to another process
questions.explanation: Thread cancellation is the process of terminating a thread before it has finished its execution.

questions.question: What is asynchronous cancellation in thread termination?
questions.option_a: A. The thread is notified and can clean up resources
questions.option_b: B. The thread continues execution until completion
questions.option_c: C. The target thread is terminated immediately
questions.option_d: D. The thread is moved to another processor
questions.explanation: Asynchronous cancellation terminates the target thread immediately, without giving it a chance to perform cleanup operations.

questions.question: What is deferred cancellation in thread termination?
questions.option_a: A. The thread is terminated immediately
questions.option_b: B. The thread continues running indefinitely
questions.option_c: C. The target thread periodically checks if it should be cancelled
questions.option_d: D. The thread is never terminated
questions.explanation: Deferred cancellation allows the target thread to periodically check if it should be cancelled, giving it the opportunity to perform cleanup operations.

questions.question: In the Pthreads example code provided, what is the purpose of pthread_join()?
questions.option_a: A. To create a new thread
questions.option_b: B. To terminate a thread immediately
questions.option_c: C. To ensure the main program waits for all threads to finish
questions.option_d: D. To detach a thread from the process
questions.explanation: pthread_join() ensures the main program waits for all threads to finish before continuing execution, allowing proper synchronization.

questions.question: In the Pthreads example, what function is executed by each created thread?
questions.option_a: A. main()
questions.option_b: B. pthread_create()
questions.option_c: C. hello()
questions.option_d: D. pthread_join()
questions.explanation: In the example, each thread executes the hello() function, which prints 'Hello Thread' and returns.

questions.question: What does the POSIX standard (IEEE 1003.1c) specify?
questions.option_a: A. The implementation details of thread libraries
questions.option_b: B. The API for thread creation and synchronization
questions.option_c: C. The hardware requirements for threading
questions.option_d: D. The scheduling algorithm for threads
questions.explanation: The POSIX standard (IEEE 1003.1c) specifies the API for thread creation and synchronization, with the implementation left to the library developers.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about the relationship between processes and threads?
questions.option_a: A. Processes run within threads
questions.option_b: B. Threads run within processes
questions.option_c: C. Processes and threads are independent entities
questions.option_d: D. Threads cannot exist within processes
questions.explanation: Threads run within processes, representing independent streams of execution within the process/application.

questions.question: What is a key characteristic of kernel threads?
questions.option_a: A. They are managed by user-level libraries
questions.option_b: B. They are supported by the Kernel
questions.option_c: C. They cannot run on multiprocessor systems
questions.option_d: D. They are always created in pairs
questions.explanation: Kernel threads are supported by the Kernel and are found in virtually all general-purpose operating systems including Windows, Linux, and Solaris.

questions.question: According to the lecture, which operating systems use kernel threads?
questions.option_a: A. Windows only
questions.option_b: B. Linux only
questions.option_c: C. Windows, Solaris, Linux, Mac OS X
questions.option_d: D. Solaris Green Threads only
questions.explanation: Kernel threads are used in virtually all general-purpose operating systems, including Windows, Solaris, Linux, Tru64 UNIX, and Mac OS X.

questions.question: What is an example of the Many-to-One threading model?
questions.option_a: A. Windows threads
questions.option_b: B. Linux threads
questions.option_c: C. Solaris Green Threads
questions.option_d: D. Mac OS X threads
questions.explanation: Solaris Green Threads and GNU Portable Threads are examples of the Many-to-One threading model, where many user threads map to a single kernel thread.

questions.question: In a multiprocessor system, how can threads execute?
questions.option_a: A. Only one thread can execute at a time
questions.option_b: B. Different threads may execute on different processors
questions.option_c: C. Threads must all execute on the same processor
questions.option_d: D. Threads cannot execute on multiprocessor systems
questions.explanation: On a multiprocessor system, different threads may execute on different processors simultaneously, enabling true parallel execution.

questions.question: Which statement about thread states is correct according to the lecture?
questions.option_a: A. Threads can only be in running or terminated states
questions.option_b: B. Threads can be in ready, blocked, running, or terminated states
questions.option_c: C. Threads have no states
questions.option_d: D. Thread states are determined by the user program
questions.explanation: Threads can be in one of several states: ready, blocked, running, or terminated, similar to process states but at a more granular level.

questions.question: What is the main difference between user threads and kernel threads?
questions.option_a: A. User threads are faster than kernel threads
questions.option_b: B. User threads are managed without kernel intervention, kernel threads are managed by the OS
questions.option_c: C. Kernel threads cannot be created
questions.option_d: D. User threads require more memory
questions.explanation: User threads are managed by user-level threads libraries without kernel intervention, while kernel threads are supported and managed by the operating system kernel.

questions.question: What does the Pthreads header file include statement look like?
questions.option_a: A. #include <threads.h>
questions.option_b: B. #include <pthread.h>
questions.option_c: C. #include <posix.h>
questions.option_d: D. #include <multithread.h>
questions.explanation: The Pthreads library is included using #include <pthread.h> to create and manage POSIX threads, as shown in the example code.

questions.question: In the context of thread pools, what does 'separating task to be performed from mechanics of creating task' allow?
questions.option_a: A. Tasks must be created immediately
questions.option_b: B. Different strategies for running tasks, such as periodic scheduling
questions.option_c: C. Tasks cannot be modified after creation
questions.option_d: D. All tasks must run immediately
questions.explanation: Separating the task from the mechanics of creating it allows different strategies for running tasks, such as scheduling tasks to run periodically.

questions.question: Which threading model is used by Solaris prior to version 9?
questions.option_a: A. Many-to-One
questions.option_b: B. One-to-One
questions.option_c: C. Many-to-Many
questions.option_d: D. One-to-Many
questions.explanation: Solaris prior to version 9 used the Many-to-Many model, which allows many user-level threads to be mapped to many kernel threads.

questions.question: What is a potential drawback of the Many-to-One threading model on multicore systems?
questions.option_a: A. Multiple threads may not run in parallel because only one may be in kernel at a time
questions.option_b: B. Threads run too fast
questions.option_c: C. Too many threads can be created
questions.option_d: D. Threads cannot communicate with each other
questions.explanation: In the Many-to-One model, multiple threads may not run in parallel on multicore systems because only one thread can be in the kernel at a time due to the single kernel thread mapping.

questions.question: What type of tasks can be implemented by separate threads in a multithreaded application?
questions.option_a: A. Only mathematical calculations
questions.option_b: B. Update display, fetch data, spell checking, answer network requests
questions.option_c: C. Only I/O operations
questions.option_d: D. Only system calls
questions.explanation: Multiple tasks within an application can be implemented by separate threads, including updating display, fetching data, spell checking, and answering network requests.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the NUM_THREADS macro in the Pthreads example?
questions.option_a: A. To limit the total threads in the system
questions.option_b: B. To set the number of threads the program will create
questions.option_c: C. To specify thread priority
questions.option_d: D. To allocate memory for threads
questions.explanation: The NUM_THREADS macro (set to 4 in the example) determines the number of threads the program will create and manage.

questions.question: According to the lecture, what is the relationship between threads and protection?
questions.option_a: A. There is strong protection between threads
questions.option_b: B. There is no protection between threads within a process
questions.option_c: C. Threads are completely isolated from each other
questions.option_d: D. Protection is enforced by hardware
questions.explanation: There is no protection between threads within a process - threads can access each other's memory, including reading or writing any other thread's stack.

questions.question: What does the scalability benefit of threads refer to?
questions.option_a: A. Threads can scale to use more memory
questions.option_b: B. The process can take advantage of multiprocessor architectures
questions.option_c: C. Threads can create unlimited child threads
questions.option_d: D. Threads can run on any operating system
questions.explanation: Scalability in threading refers to the process's ability to take advantage of multiprocessor architectures by running different threads on different processors.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid approach to signal delivery in multithreaded processes as mentioned in the lecture?
questions.option_a: A. Deliver the signal to the thread to which the signal applies
questions.option_b: B. Deliver the signal to every thread in the process
questions.option_c: C. Ignore all signals in multithreaded processes
questions.option_d: D. Assign a specific thread to receive all signals for the process
questions.explanation: Ignoring all signals is not mentioned as an approach. The lecture mentions delivering to specific threads, to every thread, to certain threads, or assigning a specific thread to receive all signals.

questions.question: What happens when pthread_create() is called in the example code?
questions.option_a: A. The main thread terminates
questions.option_b: B. A new thread is created
questions.option_c: C. All threads are joined
questions.option_d: D. The program exits
questions.explanation: pthread_create() creates a new thread, as shown in the loop where it's called for each thread ID in the array, creating multiple threads.
